title: Bṛhad Atiyāra — The Extended Confession of the Transgressions of the Twelve Lay Vows
summary: This is the long, detailed confession a Jain layperson (śrāvaka) recites to review and own up to every small slip against the vows of right living. Working vow by vow, it names dozens of concrete everyday faults — in trade, food, speech, household work, worship and devotion — and after each group the reciter says 'micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ' ('may that misdeed of mine come to nothing'). It is recited on the major periodic occasions (fortnightly, seasonal, and yearly), when the shorter daily confession is replaced by this fuller one so that nothing is overlooked.
ritualPlacement: This is the fourth of the six Āvaśyakas (obligatory acts), Paḍikkamaṇa / Pratikramaṇa — reflection and atonement — in its EXTENDED (bṛhad) form. In the daily Devasi and Rāi pratikramaṇas this slot is filled by the SHORT confession (laghu-atiyar); on the periodic occasions it is replaced by this extended text. It is recited in the Pākṣika (fortnightly), Cāturmāsika (seasonal) and Sāṁvatsarika (annual) pratikramaṇas. IMPORTANT shared-text rule, stated here once: the Cāumāsī and Sāṁvatsarī pratikramaṇas have no independent text of their own — they reuse the Pākṣika order verbatim, simply substituting the word 'pakkhī' with 'cāumāsī' or 'saṁvatsarī' and lengthening the kāyotsarga (12 Logassa for Pākṣika, 20 for Cāumāsī, 40 Logassa + 1 Navkar for Sāṁvatsarī). So this same confession serves all three occasions.
authorAndSource: Transmitted in the Anchalgachchha (Vidhipaksha Gaccha) Śvetāmbara Mūrtipūjaka recension as printed in the edition 'Panch Pratikraman Sutra – Vidhi Sahit', the golden source for this corpus. The composition is a medieval Gujarati śrāvaka-atichāra text, with its core technical lists (the five atichāras per vow, the fifteen karmādāna, the twenty-two abhakṣya, the thirty-two anantakāya, the five atichāras of saṁlekhanā) drawn from the canonical Āvaśyaka tradition; the Gujarati exposition itself is not attributed to a named author in the source book. No individual author is asserted here (see notes).
structureArc: The text moves once through the whole moral framework of the householder, vow by vow, each block ending with the same 'micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ' refrain. Verse (1) is the long preamble: it first re-roots everything in samyaktva (right faith) — confessing four-fold mithyātva (false belief: lokika/lokottara × deva-gata/guru-gata) and the five atichāras of right faith (śaṅkā, kāṅkṣā, vitigicchā, para-pāṣaṇḍa-praśaṁsā, para-pāṣaṇḍa-saṁstava), then lists the popular festivals, pilgrimages and rites to be avoided — before turning to the FIRST vow, Prāṇātipāta-viramaṇa (non-violence), with its five atichāras. Verses (2)–(5) complete the five aṇuvratas: (2) Mṛṣāvāda (truth), (3) Adattādāna (non-stealing), (4) Śīla (chastity), (5) Parigraha-parimāṇa (limiting possessions). A header then announces the three guṇavratas: (6) Diśi-vrata; (7) Bhogopabhoga in its food aspect (bhojanataḥ), which embeds the twenty-two abhakṣya and thirty-two anantakāya lists before its five atichāras; (8) Bhogopabhoga in its action aspect (karmataḥ) with the fifteen karmādāna, followed by the eighth vow, Anartha-daṇḍa. A header then announces the four śikṣāvratas: (9) Sāmāyika, (10) Deśāvagāśika, (11) Pauṣadha, (12) Atithi-saṁvibhāga. Verse (13) closes the cycle: the five atichāras of apaścima-māraṇāntika-saṁlekhanā (the final fast), the summary tally of 'eighty-five atichāras', then a review of the panchācāra (jñāna, darśana, tapa, vīrya-ācāra), the Prakrit gāthā 'Paḍisiddhāṇaṁ karaṇe…', and the concluding 'Desāvagāsiyaṁ… vosirāmi' pratyākhyāna.
recurringImagery: Rather than a single pictorial metaphor, this sutra is held together by a small set of recurring FORMULAE that repeat with each vow — understanding them once unlocks the whole text. (a) The purification formula 'e … vratataṇā pāñca atiyāra śodhuṁ' — 'I now scrutinise/cleanse the five atichāras of the Nth vow' — opens almost every block. (b) Each block lists the five canonical atichāra-names (often in Prakrit) and then expands each into vivid everyday examples. (c) The block closes with 'aneruṁ … e [Nth] vrata viṣe… te savi huṁ mane, vacane, kāyāe karī micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ' — 'and whatever else I did against this vow, by mind, speech or body, may that misdeed of mine come to nothing.' (d) The pairing sūkṣma–bādara ('subtle and gross') and jāṇatāṁ–ajāṇatāṁ ('knowingly–unknowingly') recurs to make the confession exhaustive. (e) The qualifier 'jayaṇā' ('with sparing care') marks the gap the householder leaves himself where total abstention is impossible. These devices are intentional repetitions, not redundancy — each vow is examined with the same scrupulous procedure.
keyThemes: Vow-by-vow self-examination of the householder's life
The twelve lay vows: 5 aṇuvrata + 3 guṇavrata + 4 śikṣāvrata, framed by samyaktva and capped by saṁlekhanā
Right faith (samyaktva) as the root, and the four-fold mithyātva to be shunned
Detailed atichāra (partial transgression) versus anācāra (outright breach)
Exhaustiveness: subtle and gross, known and unknown, by mind, speech and body
Jayaṇā — sparing carefulness where total restraint is impractical
Confession and release through 'micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ'
Ethical economics: forbidden trades (karmādāna) and just dealing
Dietary discipline: abhakṣya and anantakāya restrictions

glossary.definition: A partial transgression or infraction of a vow — a slip that strains the vow without fully breaking it; spelled 'atiyāra' (with ય) throughout this book.
An outright breach of a vow, more serious than an atichāra.
A 'lesser vow' — the householder's limited version of a monk's great vow; the first five of the twelve.
A 'multiplying' or merit-enhancing vow that reinforces the aṇuvratas (vows 6–8: direction-limit, enjoyment-limit, avoidance of purposeless harm).
A 'disciplinary / training' vow cultivating spiritual practice (vows 9–12: equanimity, day-limit, monastic-like retreat, charitable sharing).
A Jain layman (śrāvikā: laywoman); the householder practitioner for whom this confession is written.
Prakrit refrain: 'may my misdeed be void / forgiven' — the formal release pronounced after each block of faults.
Right faith / correct world-view; the root of the lay path, examined before the vows themselves.
False belief or wrong faith; here classified four-fold (lokika and lokottara, each deva-gata and guru-gata).
Sparing carefulness / restraint exercised where complete avoidance of harm is not feasible (Sanskrit yatanā).
The fifteen highly injurious occupations a householder should avoid (e.g. trades in fire, forest-clearing, animals, poison).
Forbidden foods — the twenty-two items (and root-vegetables) a disciplined śrāvaka does not eat.
'Infinite-bodied' beings — vegetables (the thirty-two listed) each holding innumerable souls, hence avoided.
The final, voluntary ascetic fast undertaken with equanimity at the approach of death (apaścima-māraṇāntika-saṁlekhanā), with its own five atichāras.
'Turning back' — the rite of reflection, confession and renewal of which this confession is the fourth Āvaśyaka.

howToRead: A newcomer can read this as a thorough moral checklist of lay Jain life — let the vivid examples teach what each vow protects, and treat each closing 'micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ' as a release of that group of faults. An advanced practitioner should track the fixed five-atichāra scheme per vow, the samyaktva-to-saṁlekhanā arc that totals eighty-five atichāras, and the embedded canonical lists (15 karmādāna, 22 abhakṣya, 32 anantakāya) recited verbatim within verses (1), (7) and (8).

sources.title: Panch Pratikraman Sutra – Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipaksha edition)
sources.consultedFor: Ritual placement, sūtra text, the shared-text substitution rule, and the orthography of the bṛhad-atiyar (golden source for this corpus).

sources.title: Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon (Monier-Williams)
sources.consultedFor: Sanskrit glosses for technical terms (samyaktva, mithyātva, kāyotsarga, saṁlekhanā).

sources.title: H. D. T. Sheth, Pāia-sadda-mahaṇṇavo (Prakrit–Sanskrit dictionary)
sources.consultedFor: Prakrit glosses for atichāra-names and the closing gāthā vocabulary.

notes: The source book is authoritative and its readings (including 'atiyāra' with ય and other internal spelling variants) are preserved verbatim and not normalised. The Gujarati atichāra exposition is not attributed to a named author in the source edition; only its embedded canonical lists derive from the wider Āvaśyaka tradition, so authorship is left unasserted rather than invented. The total of eighty-five atichāras (pancyāśī atiyāra) is stated within verse (13) of the text itself and corroborated by the standard count (5 samyaktva + 25 for the five aṇuvratas + 5 diśi + 5 bhogopabhoga-bhojana + 15 karmādāna + 5 anartha-daṇḍa + 5 sāmāyika + 5 deśāvagāśika + 5 pauṣadha + 5 atithi-saṁvibhāga + 5 saṁlekhanā). Web verification in this session was limited to the general structure of the twelve vows and the eighty-five-atichāra framework; deep-link URLs above point to the relevant repositories/lexica rather than to independently re-verified individual pages. Other gacchas and editions vary in wording and in some festival/food lists; this preface follows the Anchalgachchha (Vidhipaksha) recension only.
